results.possibleIdentity: Richard Adler is a media researcher, author, and consultant who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s, particularly in exploring the intersection of new technology and the arts. He is best known for editing and co-authoring several seminal works on television and electronic media, including "The Electronic Box Office: Humanities and Arts on the Cable" (1974) and "Television as a Cultural Force" (1976), both published in association with the Aspen Institute Program on Communications and Society. His work in the 1970s focused on the potential of then-emerging technologies like cable television to serve as a platform for the performing and visual arts, which aligns with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) to promote collaborations between artists and engineers. Adler served as the Director of the Teleservices Program at the Institute for the Future (IFTF) in Menlo Park and as a Distinguished Fellow at the institute. His research and publications are frequently cited in historical accounts of the 'guerrilla television' movement and early video art, documenting the collaborative efforts to leverage technical infrastructure for creative expression.

Richard Adler (1921–2012) was a prominent American composer, lyricist, and producer best known for the Tony Award-winning Broadway musicals "The Pajama Game" (1954) and "Damn Yankees" (1955). While primarily a figure of the musical theater, he was active throughout the 1960s and 1970s and was recognized as a "distinguished artist" for his cross-disciplinary work. In the early 1960s, he produced star-studded programs for the Kennedy White House and later attempted to adapt musical theater for television, such as the musical "Olympus 7-0000" (1967) for ABC Stage 67. In the 1970s and 1980s, his work moved toward symphonic compositions, including the "Yellowstone Overture" (1980) and the "Wilderness Suite" (1983), which were inspired by the American landscape and commissioned or recognized by national cultural institutions. His career often involved navigating the transition of traditional arts into new media like television and orchestral performance.

results.possibleIdentity: Stephanie Evanitsky is a pioneer in the field of aerial dance and the founder of the Multigravitational Aerodance Group (also known as the Multigravitational Experiment Group), which she established in 1969. A 1966 graduate of the Pratt Institute in Fashion, she began her dance career studying under Alwin Nikolais at the Henry Street Settlement House, where she developed an interest in the use of space and gravity. Her work, often termed 'aerodance,' utilized complex suspended apparatuses such as rope webs and mixed props to allow dancers to move in a three-dimensional environment, creating an illusion of weightlessness or zero gravity. Evanitsky was active in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1960s and 1970s, collaborating with experimental artists including video artist Woody Vasulka and musicians like Howard Romero. Her group’s performances were recognized by major institutions, including a feature in a 1973 MoMA press release and listings alongside other experimental entities like the Robert Wilson Co.
